section.title: A Good Servant of Christ Jesus
section.sectionRationale: Paul warns that in later times some will abandon the faith, following deceiving spirits, forbidding marriage and certain foods, though everything God made is good. Over against this, Timothy is to be a good servant of Christ Jesus: nourished on sound teaching, trained for godliness, an example to believers, watching his life and doctrine closely so as to save himself and his hearers.
section.thesis: Faithful ministry means rejecting ascetic error, receiving God's good gifts with thanksgiving, and training oneself for godliness, so that a young servant of Christ, by watching his life and teaching, is used to save both himself and those who hear him.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the warning against apostasy and asceticism; the call to train for godliness rather than mere physical discipline; and the charge to Timothy to set an example and watch his life and doctrine.

verses.text: Now the Spirit expressly states that in later times some will {{aphistemi|abandon the faith}} to follow deceitful spirits and the teachings of demons,
influenced by the hypocrisy of liars, whose consciences are seared with a hot iron.
They will prohibit marriage and require abstinence from certain foods that God has created to be received with thanksgiving by those who believe and know the truth.
For every {{ktisma|creation}} of God is good, and nothing that is received with thanksgiving should be rejected,
because it is sanctified by the word of God and prayer.

units.label: Train yourself for godliness
units.note: value for this life and the next

verses.text: By pointing out these things to the brothers, you will be a good servant of Christ Jesus, nourished by the words of faith and sound instruction that you have followed.
But reject irreverent, silly myths. Instead, {{gymnazo|train}} yourself for godliness.
For physical exercise is of limited value, but {{eusebeia|godliness}} is valuable in every way, holding promise for the present life and for the one to come.
This is a trustworthy saying, worthy of full acceptance.
To this end we labor and strive, because we have set our hope on the living God, who is the Savior of everyone, and especially of those who believe.

units.label: Watch your life and doctrine
units.note: set an example, do not neglect the gift

verses.text: Let no one despise your youth, but set an {{typos|example}} for the believers in speech, in conduct, in love, in faith, in purity.
Until I come, devote yourself to the public reading of Scripture, to exhortation, and to teaching.
Do not neglect the {{charisma|gift}} that is in you, which was given you through the prophecy spoken over you at the laying on of the hands of the elders.
Be diligent in these matters and absorbed in them, so that your progress will be evident to all.
Pay close attention to your life and to your teaching. Persevere in these things, for by so doing you will save both yourself and those who hear you.

aphistemi.gloss: To stand off, withdraw, or fall away; the root behind our word apostasy. The Spirit warns that some will fall away from the faith they once professed, drawn by deceiving spirits. It names a real departure, not a stumble, and sets the danger this chapter guards against.

ktisma.gloss: A created thing, a creature. Paul affirms every ktisma of God is good, echoing Genesis where God saw all He made as good. This directly refutes the ascetics who forbid marriage and foods, treating parts of God's creation as evil rather than gifts to be received with thanks.

gymnazo.gloss: To train or exercise, as an athlete in the gymnasium; the root of our word gymnasium. Timothy is to train himself for godliness with the discipline an athlete gives his body, showing that growth in godliness is deliberate, strenuous, and habitual, not passive.

eusebeia.gloss: Godliness, reverence for God lived out in daily life, a signature word of the Pastoral Epistles. Paul says it holds value for both the present life and the one to come, far surpassing the limited value of mere physical training. Godliness is the goal of all Timothy's discipline.

typos.gloss: A type, pattern, or example, an impression left that others copy. Despite his youth, Timothy is to be a typos for believers in speech, conduct, love, faith, and purity. A leader teaches not only by words but by a life others can safely imitate.

charisma.gloss: A gift of grace, an ability given by God for service. Timothy received his through prophecy and the laying on of the elders' hands. Paul warns him not to neglect it but to stir it into use, for gifts unexercised grow cold.

basic.context: The Spirit warns that some will fall away from the faith, misled by demonic teaching that forbids marriage and certain foods. Paul answers that everything God made is good and is to be received with thanksgiving. Timothy is to be a good servant of Christ Jesus, feeding on sound teaching and training himself for godliness, which is valuable for this life and the next. Though young, he is to set an example and watch his life and doctrine closely.
basic.hermeneutics: The warning about later times applies to the church's whole age, not one distant moment. The refutation of asceticism is prescriptive: God's good gifts are to be enjoyed with thanks, not treated as evil. The charge to Timothy models what every servant of Christ is to pursue, godliness, example, and perseverance in life and teaching.
basic.originalLanguages: Some will aphistemi, fall away, from the faith. Yet every ktisma, creation, of God is good. Timothy must gymnazo, train, himself for eusebeia, godliness, valuable now and forever. He is to be a typos, example, to believers and not neglect the charisma, gift, given to him.
basic.typology: The good creation received with thanksgiving looks back to Eden, where God pronounced all things good, and forward to the redeemed enjoyment of all things in the new creation. Timothy the young example prefigures the many leaders Christ raises to shepherd His people.
basic.christ: Timothy is to be a good servant of Christ Jesus, and the hope of the labor is set on the living God, the Savior of all. The ministry of reading, exhortation, and teaching centers on the words of faith, the gospel of Christ, by which people are saved.
basic.god: God is the living God who made all things good and is the Savior of everyone, especially those who believe. He gives good gifts to be received with thanksgiving, sanctified by His word and prayer. He is not a God who despises His creation but one who fills it with grace.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 1 declares all God made very good. First Corinthians 10 says receive food with thanksgiving to God. Second Timothy 1 urges Timothy to fan into flame the gift of God.
basic.oneStory: God made a good world, and error has always tried either to indulge it wrongly or to reject it as evil. Here God defends His good gifts and raises up faithful servants trained for godliness. It is part of His long work of forming a people who receive His grace with thanks and hold fast the gospel through every generation.

context.literary: Chapter 4 opens with a solemn Spirit-given prophecy of apostasy, then swings to the positive: what makes a good servant of Christ Jesus. A trustworthy saying in verse 9 punctuates the call to godliness, and the chapter ends with a cluster of imperatives to Timothy, climaxing in the charge to watch his life and doctrine.
context.historical: The ascetic errors, forbidding marriage and foods, reflect early proto-gnostic and rigorist tendencies that treated matter as evil. Timothy was relatively young for such authority, so Paul addresses the danger of his being despised and urges him to lead by example, including the public reading of Scripture central to early worship.
context.canonical: The passage stands with Colossians 2 against asceticism and with 2 Timothy in charging a young minister to guard and exercise his gift. Its affirmation that all creation is good anchors a Christian view of the body, marriage, and food.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn against ascetic false teaching, to reaffirm the goodness of creation received with thanksgiving, and to charge Timothy to train for godliness and to guard his life and doctrine so that he and his hearers are saved.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The prophecy of falling away describes what will happen; the responses are prescriptive: reject asceticism, receive God's gifts with thanks, train for godliness, and persevere. The specific errors are examples of a wider pattern of teaching that rejects God's good creation.

debates.question: How is God the Savior of everyone, especially believers, in verse 10?

views.label: Savior in a general and a saving sense
views.case: God is the preserver and benefactor of all people, giving life and good gifts to everyone, yet the Savior in the full, eternal sense only of those who believe.

views.label: Christ's atonement sufficient for all, effective for believers
views.case: The death of Christ is of infinite worth, offered to all, but savingly applied to those who trust Him, so He is especially the Savior of believers.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold that salvation in the full sense comes only through faith, while affirming God's genuine goodness toward all people.

debates.question: What does you will save both yourself and your hearers mean in verse 16?

views.label: Perseverance as the path of assurance
views.case: Salvation is by grace, but God saves through means; by persevering in sound life and doctrine Timothy walks the road on which God keeps him and blesses his hearers.

views.label: The instrumental role of faithful ministry
views.case: Timothy does not save anyone by his own power, but God uses faithful ministers as the means through whom the gospel reaches and rescues others.

debates.bearing: Neither view makes Timothy the author of salvation; both stress that God saves through the persevering, faithful ministry of the gospel.

study.originalLanguages: The Spirit foretells that some will aphistemi, stand away or fall from the faith, the root of apostasy. Against those who reject parts of creation, Paul insists every ktisma, created thing, of God is good, echoing Genesis. Timothy must gymnazo, train, himself as an athlete for eusebeia, godliness, which unlike mere bodily exercise holds value for this life and the next. Though young, he is to be a typos, a pattern or example, for believers, and he must not neglect the charisma, the grace-gift, given him through prophecy and the elders' hands. The chapter presses discipline, example, and perseverance as the marks of a good servant of Christ Jesus.

typology.type: The good creation received with thanksgiving
typology.fulfillment: Looks back to Eden's very good world and forward to the redeemed enjoyment of all things in the new creation.

typology.type: Timothy the young example and steward of a gift
typology.fulfillment: Prefigures the many faithful leaders Christ raises up and equips to shepherd and teach His people.

study.christ: Timothy's whole aim is to be a good servant of Christ Jesus, nourished on the words of faith which are the gospel of Christ. The labor of ministry rests its hope on the living God, the Savior of all, whose salvation comes through faith in His Son. The public reading of Scripture, exhortation, and teaching all serve to hold Christ before the people, and it is by this gospel ministry that hearers are saved.
study.god: God is the living God, the Maker who pronounced all things good and the Savior of everyone, especially of those who believe. He gives marriage and food and every gift to be received with thanksgiving, sanctified by His word and prayer. He is no despiser of His creation, and He equips His servants with gifts of grace to be exercised for the good of His people.

crossReferences.note: God saw all that He had made, and it was very good, the ground for receiving creation with thanks.
A parallel rebuke of ascetic rules that have an appearance of wisdom but no value against the flesh.
If I partake with thankfulness, and do all to the glory of God, echoing verses 4 and 5.
Fan into flame the gift of God, the same charge not to neglect the charisma.

study.oneStory: God made a good world, and error has always tried either to abuse it or to reject it as evil. Here God defends His good gifts and raises up servants trained for godliness who guard the gospel. Timothy's discipline and example belong to God's long work of forming a people who receive His grace with thanksgiving and pass on sound teaching, generation to generation, until Christ appears.

quiz.q: How does Paul answer those who forbid marriage and certain foods?

options.text: Every creation of God is good and is to be received with thanksgiving.
options.note: Verses 4 and 5 affirm the goodness of what God made.

options.text: Such rules make Christians holier than others.
options.note: Paul treats these prohibitions as demonic teaching, not a path to holiness.

options.text: Food and marriage are matters of no importance.
options.note: They are good gifts to be received with thanks, not trivial.

options.text: The physical world is inherently evil.
options.note: Paul says the opposite: everything God made is good.

quiz.q: What does Paul urge Timothy to train himself for?

options.text: Godliness, which is valuable for this life and the next.
options.note: Verses 7 and 8 contrast godliness with mere physical exercise.

options.text: Physical fitness above all else.
options.note: Bodily exercise has limited value; godliness is valuable in every way.

options.text: Winning popularity with the crowds.
options.note: The goal is godliness, not popularity.

options.text: Mastering the silly myths of the false teachers.
options.note: Paul tells him to reject those myths entirely.

quiz.q: How is Timothy to handle being young in a place of leadership?

options.text: By setting an example in speech, conduct, love, faith, and purity.
options.note: Verse 12 tells him to be an example so no one despises his youth.

options.text: By hiding until he is older.
options.note: Paul calls him to lead by example now, not to withdraw.

options.text: By ruling harshly to prove himself.
options.note: The call is to godly example, not heavy-handedness.

options.text: By abandoning his gift as premature.
options.note: He is told not to neglect the gift but to use it.

quiz.q: What is Timothy to do with the gift given to him?

options.text: Not neglect it, but be diligent and absorbed in exercising it.
options.note: Verses 14 and 15 urge active use of the gift.

options.text: Keep it hidden so as not to seem proud.
options.note: The danger named is neglect, not overuse.

options.text: Trade it for a more impressive gift.
options.note: He is to use the gift he was given, not exchange it.

options.text: Assume it will grow on its own without effort.
options.note: Gifts unexercised grow cold; Paul urges diligence.

quiz.q: What does Paul say results from watching one's life and doctrine closely?

options.text: By persevering, Timothy will save both himself and those who hear him.
options.note: Verse 16 links perseverance in life and teaching to salvation.

options.text: He will earn salvation by his own effort.
options.note: Salvation is by grace; God saves through faithful means, not by earning.

options.text: Only his hearers benefit, never himself.
options.note: Paul says both himself and his hearers.

options.text: Careful attention makes no real difference.
options.note: Paul insists it is the path God uses to save.

questions: Paul says every good gift is to be received with thanksgiving. Where might you be either despising God's good gifts or grasping them without thanks?
Godliness, like fitness, is trained, not wished into being. What deliberate habits are you building to grow in godliness this season?
Timothy was to be an example despite his youth. In which area, speech, conduct, love, faith, or purity, is your example most needed right now?
Paul warns against neglecting the gift within. What has God gifted you to do that you have been letting go cold, and how will you stir it up?
Watch your life and your doctrine, Paul says. Which of the two, how you live or what you believe, are you more tempted to neglect, and why?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote records the variant and suffer reproach for and strive, reflecting differences among manuscript families.
Train yourself renders gymnazo, athletic training language, picturing godliness as strenuous, disciplined exercise, not passive drift.
Save both yourself and your hearers speaks of God saving through faithful, persevering ministry; it does not make the minister the author of salvation.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Timothy 4:1-16 this session. No em or en dashes occur in this passage.
